claim: The Alzheimer's Association organized a broad campaign (with Congress, state AGs, clinicians and grassroots advocates) to overturn CMS's registry-restricted Medicare coverage of anti-amyloid antibodies - a clear case of patient advocacy shaping drug ACCESS, and a divergence from the skeptical KOL record.
priority: broaden Medicare access to FDA-approved anti-amyloid antibodies (oppose registry-as-barrier)
quote: But following extensive efforts by the Alzheimer's Association, bipartisan members of Congress, state attorneys general, clinicians and, most importantly, advocates from communities all across the country, CMS has

claim: FDA issued ALS-specific drug-development guidance (Sept 2019) creating a tailored benefit-risk/endpoint framework for this fatal CNS disease - a regulatory accommodation the unusually organized ALS patient community pressed for (FDA's patient-focused benefit-risk in practice).
quote: The purpose of this guidance is to assist sponsors in the clinical development of drugs and biological products for the treatment of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis
tier_note: fda.gov primary (T1). Verbatim quote supports the guidance's existence/purpose; the patient-advocacy framing (community pressed for ALS-specific flexibility) is context, widely documented.

claim: Eteplirsen's 2016 accelerated approval - 'the first oligonucleotide to be approved based on very limited data,' approval 'shrouded in controversy' - is the textbook case in which intense DMD patient/family advocacy is widely credited with influencing an FDA decision over internal staff objections.
priority: accelerated access to a first DMD therapy on a surrogate (dystrophin)
attribution: Lim KRQ & Yokota commentary (documents the limited-data controversy); advocacy role widely reported
quote: it is the first drug that is approved for DMD in the United States, the first approved oligonucleotide that modulates splicing, the first approved PMO, and also the first oligonucleotide to be approved based on very limited data.
tier_note: Quote anchors the limited-data/controversy (the advocacy-era landmark); the specific advocacy-influenced-FDA framing is context, not asserted as the commentary's verbatim.

claim: The Michael J. Fox Foundation (Parkinson's) publicly reports deploying tens of millions (e.g. $62.4M) into tools to track PD and new therapies - a marker of the scale of organized, well-funded CNS patient advocacy that shapes the research agenda.
org: The Michael J. Fox Foundation for Parkinson's Research
priority: fund PD biomarkers/progression tools and disease-modifying therapies
quote: $62.4M for Tools to Track PD and Therapies

claim: EMET grounding of ALS advocacy urgency: ALS typically kills within ~3 years of symptom onset, and riluzole (long the only effective drug) extends survival just 3-6 months - the quantified unmet need that underpins the organized intensity of ALS patient advocacy.
attribution: Hardiman, van den Berg & Kiernan 2011 (Nat Rev Neurol) - via EMET
quote: Patients typically die from respiratory failure within 3 years of symptom onset. ... Riluzole remains the only effective drug, and extends the average survival of patients by 3-6 months.
